title: ACER Decision No 07-2025 on the European Resource Adequacy Assessment (ERAA) 2024
text:
PUBLIC
DECISION No 07/2025
OF THE EUROPEAN UNION AGENCY
FOR THE COOPERATION OF ENERGY REGULATORS
of 11 August 2025
on the European Resource Adequacy Assessment for 2024
THE EUROPEAN UNION AGENCY FOR THE COOPERATION OF ENERGY
REGULATORS,
Having regard to the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union,
Having regard to Regulation (EU) 2019/942 of the European Parliament and of the Council of
5 June 2019 establishing a European Union Agency for the Cooperation of Energy Regulators1,
and, in particular, Article 9(1)(a) thereof,
Having regard to Regulation (EU) 2019/943 of the European Parliament and of the Council of
5 June 2019 on the internal market for electricity2, and, in particular, Article 23(7) and Article
27 thereof,
Having regard to the outcome of the consultation with the European Network of Transmission
System Operators for Electricity,
Having regard to the outcome of the consultation with ACER’s Electricity Working Group,
Having regard to the favourable opinion of the Board of Regulators of 7 August 2025, delivered
pursuant to Article 22(5)(a) of Regulation (EU) 2019/942,
Whereas:
1. INTRODUCTION
(1) The European Resource Adequacy Assessment (ERAA) is a pan-European
assessment of power system resource adequacy up to 10 years ahead, aiming to model
and analyse possible events which can adversely impact the balance between supply
1 OJ L 158, 14.6.2019, p. 22.
2 OJ L 158, 14.6.2019, p. 54.
Page 1 of 42

Decision No 07/2025
and demand of electric power. The European Network of Transmission System
Operators for Electricity (ENTSO-E) carries out ERAA on an annual basis.
(2) The purpose of annual ERAAs is to identify resource adequacy concerns, and to
provide a robust and objective basis for policy decisions, in particular when assessing
the need for capacity mechanisms. As such, ERAA has a major role in resource
adequacy policies.
(3) Annual ERAAs must be based on the methodology for the European resource
adequacy assessment (ERAA methodology),3 which was developed by ENTSO-E
under Article 23(3) of Regulation (EU) 2019/943 (Electricity Regulation) and
approved on 2 October 2020 by ACER Decision No 24/2020.
(4) On 7 April 2025, ENTSO-E submitted for assessment to ACER its 2024 proposal of
scenarios, sensitivities, assumptions and results as required by Article 23(7) of the
Electricity Regulation (collectively ERAA 2024 or the Report). On 28 April 2025,
ENTSO-E completed its submission upon ACER’s request.
(5) This Decision contains nine annexes. Annexes I.a – I.g set out the amended ERAA
2024, Annex II contains ACER’s amendments to ERAA 2024 in track changes, and
Annex III (‘technical annex’) supplements ACER’s assessment in section 6 by
providing a technical review of specific elements of ERAA 2024.
(6) In its ERAA 2023 Decision, ACER emphasised the need for improved consistency of
the annual ERAA, that is, using same assumptions in both ERAA modules. In
particular, ACER stressed the need to adopt more robust approaches to the investment
modelling, apply flow-based capacity calculation method to estimate cross-zonal
capacities and enhance the modelling of investor behaviour4. The Decision also
requested ENTSO-E to perform an analysis of the curtailment-sharing feature5.
(7) ERAA 2024 is a step forward in the continuous effort to deliver a robust, reliable tool
for the European decision-makers. It also marks a development in the ongoing
commitment to implement the ERAA methodology. Since the deadline to implement
the ERAA methodology has already passed, ENTSO-E’s commitment to align the
annual ERAAs with the European methodology should remain firm. For the next
ERAA, ACER also recommends that ENTSO-E solidifies the advancements made so
far by transitioning temporary solutions into permanent ones.
3 Annex I to ACER Decision No 24/2020.
4 Decision No 06/2024.
5 Curtailment sharing is explained in section 6.2.5 of this Decision.
Page 2 of 42

Decision No 07/2025
2. PROCEDURE
Engagement with ENTSO-E and other parties before the submission of ERAA
2024
(8) ACER has been engaging with ENTSO-E on ERAA 2024 since January 2024 in
preparation for the formal submission in April 2025. This allowed ACER to provide
feedback in the early stages of the development phase of ERAA 2024, rather than only
reviewing the submitted version. In particular:
(9) In a letter of 13 March 2024, ACER and the regulatory authorities urged ENTSO-E
and all EU transmission system operators (TSOs) to prioritise consistency of the
assumptions and modelling approaches in ERAA 2024. ACER also addressed this at
the ERAA webinar hosted by ENTSO-E in August 2024.
(10) On 27 March 2024, ACER submitted its reaction on the preliminary input data
publicly consulted by ENTSO-E, where consistency needed to be improved including
the data on cross-zonal capacities, demand projections and assumptions regarding
demand-side response (DSR).
(11) Throughout the ERAA 2024 development process, ACER shared with ENTSO-E its
views on some challenging implementation aspect intended to support the
methodology application. Particularly, in July 2024, ACER provided the notes
clarifying the scope of the curtailment sharing study expected from ENTSO-E in the
course of the year and offering methodological insights on the selection of weather
scenarios.
(12) In parallel, ACER regularly discussed ERAA 2024 with the regulatory authorities,
through ACER’s Security of Supply Task Force and Electricity Working Group, and
with the European Commission and the Member States, through the Electricity
Coordination Group. ACER also worked with the Commission’s Joint Research
Centre to get expert advice on handling methodological challenges in ERAA.
Proceedings following the submission of ERAA 2024
(13) On 7 April 2025, ENTSO-E published the draft ERAA 20246 and submitted it for
ACER’s approval. The submission consisted of an Executive Report describing the
purpose of the assessment and its main findings and the ERAA 2024’s input dataset
along with six annexes:
Annex 1 Assumptions
Annex 2 Methodology
6 See ENTSO-E’s European Resource Adequacy Assessment 2024 Edition.
Page 3 of 42

Decision No 07/2025
Annex 3 Detailed results
Annex 4 Case Study – Application of Revenue-Based EVA Approach on
European Scale
Annex 5 Country comments
Annex 6 Definition and glossary
(14) On 28 April 2025, following ACER’s request of 11 April to complete the submission,
ENTSO-E submitted the results for Italy and Malta for the year 2026.
(15) On 5 May 2025, ACER issued a public notice about the initiation of its decision
procedure.
(16) From 5 May to 16 June 2025, ACER reviewed the submission and communicated with
ENTSO-E to request clarifications and further information.
(17) On 16 June 2025, ACER shared its preliminary position on the draft ERAA 2024 with
ENTSO-E and the Member States via the Electricity Coordination Group, inviting
views by 27 June 2025.
(18) On 30 June 2025, ENTSO-E provided its written views and further information, and
complemented it during an oral hearing on 4 July 2025. ACER also received written
views from the Italian Ministry. The received views are summarised in section 4.11.
(19) On 14 July 2025, ENTSO-E submitted additional information, upon ACER’s request,
to further complement the oral hearing. Following that, ACER closed the oral and
written procedure.
(20) The AEWG was consulted on ACER’s draft Decision between 15-22 July 2025 and
provided its advice on 22 July 2025 (see section 4.2).
(21) ACER’s Board of Regulators issued a favourable opinion on 7 August 2025
3. ACER’S COMPETENCE TO DECIDE ON ERAA 2024
(22) Article 9(1)(a) of Regulation (EU) 2019/942 states that ACER shall approve and
amend, where necessary, the proposals for calculations related to ERAA pursuant to
Article 23(7) of the Electricity Regulation.
(23) Pursuant to Article 23(7) of the Electricity Regulation, ERAA’s scenarios,
sensitivities, assumptions and results shall be subject to the prior consultation of the
Member States, the ECG and the relevant stakeholders, and approval by ACER under
the procedure set out in Article 27 of the Electricity Regulation. As specified in Article
27, ACER has three months to either approve or amend ERAA. In the latter case,
ACER shall consult ENTSO-E before approving the amended ERAA.
Page 4 of 42

Decision No 07/2025
(24) On 7 April 2025, ENTSO-E submitted ERAA 2024 (including scenarios, sensitivities,
assumptions and results) to ACER for approval and completed its submission on 28
April 2025. ACER is competent to decide on ERAA 2024 based on Article 9(1)(a) of
Regulation (EU) 2019/942 and Articles 23(7) and 27 of the Electricity Regulation.
4. SUMMARY OF THE OBSERVATIONS RECEIVED BY ACER
Consultation on ACER’s preliminary position
(25) This section summarises the views of ENTSO-E and the Member States consulted on
ACER’s preliminary position.
4.1.1. Feedback from ENTSO-E
(26) Overall, ENTSO-E welcomed ACER’s preliminary position to approve ERAA 2024.
Its detailed feedback focused on three areas: methodological choices, curtailment
sharing and national considerations pertaining to France, Belgium, Italy and Malta.
(27) First, according to ENTSO-E, ERAA 2024 incorporates the best methodological
choices available, considering time and process constraints. The applied approach,
ENTSO-E argues, meets the standards for robustness, transparency and technical
soundness. For ACER’s position, see, in particular, section 6.2.5.
(28) Second, ENTSO-E disagrees with using pre-curtailment sharing outcomes as final
adequacy indicators for ERAA 2024, as it would misrepresent the adequacy risks. In
its feedback, ENTSO-E advocates for complete, technically robust outcomes resulting
from the application of curtailment sharing feature. For ACER’s position, see, in
particular, sections 6.2.5 and 7.2.
(29) Third, in ENTSO-E’s view, ERAA 2024 reflects the extensive data collection and
stakeholder engagement. Hence, ENTSO-E urged ACER to maintain methodological
choices regarding Italy and Malta’s 2026 results and improved modelling for Belgium
and France. For ACER’s position, see, in particular, sections 6.2.1, 6.2.5 and 7.2.
4.1.2. Feedback from the Italian Ministry
(30) The Italian Ministry also submitted written views on ACER’s preliminary position,
stating that the 2026 results for Italy do not correspond to the current operational
conditions of the national power system. The Ministry remarked that the publication
of the 2026 results should be accompanied by an explicit acknowledgment of the
reasons for ENTSO-E to remove them from ERAA 2024. For ACER’s position, see
in particular sections 6.2.1 and 7.2.
Consultation of ACER’s Electricity Working Group
(31) The AEWG provided its advice on 22 July 2025 and endorsed the draft Decision. It
appreciates the amendments to the ERAA 2024, commenting that they provide clarity,
transparency and a better explanation of the results. Three regulatory authorities
Page 5 of 42

Decision No 07/2025
provided written comments during the consultation phase. The AEWG invited ACER
to consider these comments.
(32) CRE (France) recognised that the amendments provide very valuable insights to the
study. CRE commented on additional French nuclear availability profiles introduced
in ERAA 2024 and serving as a proof of concept following ACER’s amendment (see
sections 6.2.5 and 7.2). CRE requested reiterating in section 7.2 that the results with
additional profiles can have a value in the interpretation of the ERAA results.
(33) BNetzA (Germany) emphasised the impact of curtailment sharing on the adequacy
results and the importance of the analysis requested in ERAA 2023 Decision, which
ENTSO-E has not delivered yet. Moreover, BNetzA stressed that full internal
consistency of the model brings about computational complexity issue in the current
setup of two ERAA modules. BNetzA suggested that the input in both modules of
ERAA should be adequately similar.
(34) CRU (Ireland) stated that it should be clarified that the adequacy results presented in
the Executive Report for some countries, including Ireland, include the consideration
of out-of-market measures and that the outcomes without those measures are
presented in the dedicated annex.
(35) ACER incorporated all those views in the Decision.
(36) The AEWG also expressed its support for the ACER’s recommendations towards
improvements in the upcoming and future ERAA.
5. LEGAL FRAMEWORK
(37) The relevant provisions governing ERAA are set out in Chapter IV of the Electricity
Regulation and the ERAA methodology. Section 5.1 explains the purpose of ERAA,
particularly considering the principles of electricity market operation outlined in
Article 3 of the Electricity Regulation. Section 5.2 briefly summarises the legal
requirements for ERAA set out in Chapter IV of the Electricity Regulation.
ERAA’s purpose
(38) Pursuant to Recitals (43) and (44) of the Electricity Regulation, ENTSO-E should
carry out a robust ERAA to provide an objective basis for the assessment of resource
adequacy concerns in the EU. ERAA is mainly used to identify resource adequacy
concerns and to assess the need for capacity mechanisms. As such, adequacy concerns
to be addressed by capacity mechanisms should be primarily identified in ERAA. The
European assessment may be complemented by national resource adequacy
assessments. ERAA is expected to provide insights for decisions relevant for
achieving the objectives of the Energy Union, including an increased efficiency,
higher share of renewable energy sources, security of supply, flexibility,
sustainability, decarbonisation and innovation.
(39) In the context of the market operation principles, ERAA should consider the relevant
market trends which have been driven by market integration, free price formation,
Page 6 of 42

Decision No 07/2025
decarbonisation of the electricity system through the deployment of renewable energy
and energy efficiency, increased system flexibility and development of DSR including
consumer empowerment and other principles.7 ERAA should also consider that,
pursuant to these principles, barriers to cross-zonal electricity exchanges will
progressively be removed.8 Finally, according to these principles, market entry and
exit should be based on undertakings’ assessments of the economic and financial
viability of their operations.9
Legal requirements for ERAA
(40) Pursuant to Article 23(1) of the Electricity Regulation, first sentence, ERAA shall
identify resource adequacy concerns by assessing the overall adequacy of the
electricity system to supply current and projected demands for electricity at Union
level, at the level of the Member States, and at the level of individual bidding zones,
where relevant.
(41) Articles 23(1) and 23(5) lay down the relevant requirements for annual ERAA,
prescribing the scope, contents and methods. These requirements are reflected in
Articles 1, 3, 4, 6, 7 and 8 of the ERAA methodology.
(42) Articles 23(2), 23(4), 23(7), 27(2) and 27(3) of the Electricity Regulation set out the
requirements for the ERAA process, including a mandatory consultation. Moreover,
Articles 30(1)(c), 31 and 41(2) of the Electricity Regulation require that ENTSO-E
operates in full transparency and ensures appropriate stakeholder engagement. These
requirements are reflected in Articles 5, 9, 10 and 11 of the ERAA methodology.
(43) Pursuant to Article 27(3) in joint reading with Article 23(7) of the Electricity
Regulation, ACER has three months from the submission date to either approve or
amend ERAA. ACER shall publish the approved ERAA on its website.
6. ASSESSMENT OF ERAA 2024
Broader context and ACER’s assessment approach
(44) The European power system is undergoing a significant transformation, with fossil
fuel-based generation accounting for just 33 % of the EU’s power generation in 2024,
the lowest share on record10. At the same time, an increasing number of low-carbon
energy sources, many of them variable, are being connected to European grids. This
shift aligns with the EU’s broader electrification and decarbonisation goals,
emphasising the importance of maintaining a constant balance between supply and
demand. In this evolving landscape, flexibility solutions such as DSR and battery
storage will play a crucial role in ensuring security of supply. ERAA is aiming to
7 Points (c), (d), (e), (f), (g), (j), (l) and (m) of Article 3 of the Electricity Regulation.
8 Point (h) of Article 3 of the Electricity Regulation.
9 Point (n) of Article 3 of the Electricity Regulation.
10 According the 2025 ACER Monitoring Report on Key developments in European electricity and gas markets
Page 7 of 42

Decision No 07/2025
account for these dynamics to deliver reliable forecasts and inform future steps
regarding security of supply of the European electricity system.
(45) As per ACER methodology11, ERAA aims at ensuring European coordination,
accurate and efficient use of resources, as well as transparent modelling of resource
adequacy. Deviations from the methodology may negatively impact the quality of the
assessment, and ultimately could lead to ill-informed policy decisions, affecting the
future of the European electricity system. To ensure that ERAA can serve its purpose
as a well-functioning instrument for policymaking, ACER evaluates if any
simplifications or deviations from the methodology risk compromising the accuracy
and reliability of the assessment.
(46) Drawing on the considerations above, ACER assesses the main components of ERAA
2024 in the following sections. Where relevant, the reader is referred to Annex III
where certain aspects are assessed in more detail. A table summarising ACER’s
assessment is provided in Section 7.1.
Assessment of substantive requirements
6.2.1. Geographical scope of ERAA 2024
(47) Article 23(1) of the Electricity Regulation provides that adequacy is assessed at Union
level, at Member State level, and at the individual bidding zone level, where relevant.
Article 23(5)(a) of the Electricity Regulation further requires that ERAA is carried out
at each bidding zone level covering at least all Member States. This requirement is
further specified in Article 1 and Article 4 of the ERAA methodology.
(48) ERAA 2024 explicitly models 26 interconnected EU Member States12 based on the
current bidding zone delineation. It also provides results for all individual Member
States and bidding zones except for Italy and Malta, for which ENTSO-E excluded
the results for the 2026 target year. According to ENTSO-E, the excluded results are
inconsistent with the latest events observed in Italy, that is low adequacy margins
reported in early September 2024. The submitted report concludes that, based on this
case, there could be a difference between the adequacy modelling approaches and the
dynamics observed in the TSOs’ control areas.13
(49) Without the results for the Italian and Maltese bidding zones for 2026, the ERAA
2024 results would be incomplete and would not comply with Articles 23(1) and
23(5)(a) of the Electricity Regulation. Therefore, ACER requested ENTSO-E to
complete the submission by providing the results for both countries.
11 Methodology for the European resource adequacy assessment, 2020
12 ERAA 2024 models Cyprus non-explicitly in accordance with Article 64(2) of the Electricity Regulation.
13 It is worth to note that ERAA dimensions capacities in a cost-optimal manner to exactly match the demand.
Page 8 of 42

Decision No 07/2025
(50) ACER notes that ERAA identified no adequacy concerns in the Italian zones for 2026.
ACER finds that the results are broadly in line with the findings of ERAA 2023 central
reference scenario.
6.2.2. Temporal scope of ERAA 2024
(51) Article 23(1) of the Electricity Regulation requires that ERAA covers each year within
a period of ten years from the date of assessment; this essentially means the period
2025-2034 for ERAA 2024. Article 4 of the ERAA methodology further specifies the
temporal scope of the assessment by defining the study time period and setting out the
requirements to simulate each target year in that period.
(52) ERAA 2024 models three target years within the study period: 2026, 2028 and 2030.
Moreover, the assessment also covers 2035, a year beyond the 10-year period required
by the Electricity Regulation, but one that may correspond to policy targets or other
planning instruments, such as TYNDP14.
(53) As noted in previous ERAA decisions,15 this simplification impacts the functionality
of ERAA 2024. The assessment only identifies resource adequacy concerns for the
four years modelled in detail but cannot do so for the remaining years (see section
5.1). Nevertheless, ERAA 2024 covers most of the years relevant for decision making
on capacity mechanisms.16 The omission of other years has no impact on the accuracy
of the provided results. Therefore, ACER considers that this simplification is
acceptable for ERAA 2024.
6.2.3. Scenario framework
(54) According to Article 23(5) of the Electricity Regulation, the annual ERAA should be
based on appropriate central reference scenarios and appropriate sensitivities.
(55) Article 3 of the ERAA methodology specifies that ERAA must rely on two central
reference scenarios: one scenario considering the impact of approved capacity
mechanisms and another scenario excluding capacity mechanisms (except for
contracts already awarded at the time of the assessment). A central reference scenario
is supposed to represent the most likely future development of the electricity system
considering energy and decarbonation policies objectives. According to Article 8(1)
of the ERAA methodology it has a distinctive function, as ENTSO-E identifies a
resource adequacy concern only if the reliability standard is not fulfilled for the target
year for at least one central reference scenario.
14 TYNDP 2024, Infrastructure Gaps Report.
15 For instance, Decision No 06/2024.
16 For example, the modelled year 2028 is important for the introduction of new market-wide capacity mechanisms
or the signing of contracts for existing market-wide capacity mechanisms. Market-wide capacity mechanisms
usually consist of a main auction four years in advance of delivery (the so-called T-4 auction) and a
complementary auction one year in advance of delivery (the so-called T-1 auction).
Page 9 of 42

Decision No 07/2025
(56) ERAA 2024 only provides the latter of the two mandatory central reference scenarios
and does not consider the scenario with capacity mechanisms. The absence of a
scenario with capacity mechanisms adversely impacts the full functionality of ERAA.
First, it does not take into consideration how capacity mechanisms enhance security
of supply in the Member States where they already exist. Second, how the benefits of
additional capacity brought forward through already approved capacity mechanisms
in one Member State extend to other Member States. By overlooking the impact of
existing capacity mechanisms, ERAA 2024 provides only a partial assessment of
future adequacy risks. Since the assessment can still be used to identify adequacy
concerns, ACER considers this simplification acceptable in ERAA 2024.
6.2.4. Greenhouse gas emissions reduction target
(57) Article 23(5) of the Electricity Regulation requires that ERAA is based on appropriate
central reference scenarios of projected demand and supply, including measures to
reach energy efficiency targets. Article 3 of the ERAA methodology further specifies
that the central reference scenarios must be in line with the national objectives and
targets in National Energy and Climate Plans (NECPs). Further details on ACER’s
assessment regarding the alignment of reference scenarios with greenhouse gas
emission targets are provided in the chapter 2 of Annex III to this Decision.
(58) The EU’s greenhouse gas emissions target is set by the fit-for-55 framework, binding
the EU to reduce emissions by at least 55% from the 1990 levels by 203017. Following
the REPowerEU Plan, the framework was updated to effectively target a 57 %
reduction in emissions18. The Member States are required to integrate the latest fit-
for-55 framework in their regularly updated NECPs. ACER notes that the final
updated NECPs were published after the data collection of ERAA 2024 and could not
have been fully considered by the TSOs and ENTSO-E in the assumptions for ERAA
2024. However, the analysis offers valuable insights into the EU’s current direction
towards meeting the fit-for-55 target for renewable energy. At the time of the ERAA
2024 submission, 24 Member States had submitted their final updated NECPs after
implementing the Commission’s recommendations19.
(59) ERAA 2024 broadly aligns with the fit-for-55 target for renewable energy. In total,
the assumed renewable capacity of wind and solar in 2030 has increased by 19 GW
compared to ERAA 2023 and is in line with the NECPs (3 % higher for wind and 0.6
% higher for solar than the 2030 targets). However, the level of alignment varies
across Member States. The installed renewable capacity in ERAA 2024 exceeds the
17 Regulation (EU) 2021/1119 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 30 June 2021 establishing the
framework for achieving climate neutrality and amending Regulations (EC) No 401/2009 and (EU) 2018/1999,
OJ L 243, 9.7.2021, p. 1.
18 According to the European Commission on the fit-for-55 targets: Completion of key ‘Fit for 55' legislation
19 By the time of the complete submission of ERAA 2024, 28/04/2025, the three Member States Belgium, Estonia
and Poland had not yet submitted the final updated NECP. Hence their draft updated NECPs are used for this
analysis. See more information on the timeline for updating the NECPs: European Commission’s National energy
and climate plans page.
Page 10 of 42

Decision No 07/2025
NECP in half of the studied Member States, while the assumptions in the other half of
Member States in total are below the NECP20. ACER notes that most discrepancies
are relatively small, resulting in an overall consistency of ERAA with the EU target,
and that substantial deviations occur in a small number of countries21.
(60) With those considerations in mind, ACER acknowledges that ERAA 2024 broadly
aligns with the current EU ambitions regarding the development of renewables. ACER
sees there is still a scope to bring the scenario assumptions closer to the targets,
particularly for Member States with notably lower projections. Such alignment is
feasible in ERAA 2025, where every final updated NECPs will be accounted for in
the assumptions.
6.2.5. Economic viability assessment
(61) Article 23(5) of the Electricity Regulation requires that the central reference scenarios
include an economic viability assessment (EVA) of the likelihood of retirement,
mothballing and new-build of generation assets, and that ERAA appropriately
considers the contribution of all existing and potential future resources. The EVA-
related requirements are further specified in Articles 3 to 7 of the ERAA methodology.
Additional details on ACER’s assessment of the EVA are provided in chapter 3 of
Annex III to this Decision.
(62) The EVA module keeps the stochastic model formulation as in ERAA 2022 and
ERAA 2023, i.e. it seeks to minimise the total system cost as a probabilistic
formulation of the costs incurred in all examined years. Following ACER’s
recommendation,22 ENTSO-E has also conducted a pilot project using an iterative
revenues-based approach for the EVA, both approaches being in line with Article
6(2)(b) of the ERAA methodology. The outcomes of this pilot project are not included
in the results of ERAA 2024.
(63) The ERAA 2024 EVA explicitly models four target years, and similarly to ERAA
2023, the intermediate years are not modelled explicitly. It is assumed that
intermediate target years are duplicates of the latest available target year. Hence, the
EVA does not fully capture the system dynamics of the intermediate years within the
ERAA horizon, which may influence decisions regarding entry and exit (or
mothballing and life-extension) of capacity, and the corresponding impact on
adequacy risks. This approach, which relies on duplication rather than interpolation,
may underestimate the volume of investments that would be economically viable to
20 ACER was not able to compare the values for Cyprus, Luxembourg and Malta.
21 These conclusions are supported by the analysis of the ERAA 2024 assumptions against the Member State
NECPs. ACER’s analysis shows that the ERAA 2024 assumptions for Finland, Lithuania and the Netherlands are
considerably above the NECPs, while for Belgium, Bulgaria, France, Poland and Portugal they are considerably
below the renewable energy goals set by the Member States, with reference to 2030. For more information see
chapter 2 of Annex III to this Decision.
22 Decision No 06/2024.
Page 11 of 42

Decision No 07/2025
meet the growing demand during those intermediate years, or capacities that would
exit the market due to unprofitable market conditions.
(64) In ERAA 2024, ENTSO-E updated the climate database used to determine the inputs
of the ED and the EVA, introducing the new Pan-European Climate Database 4.1 This
update improves climate change representation as it is based on future projections
(referred to as weather scenarios) instead of detrended historical data (referred to as
climate years). Another change is the shift from 35 climate years to 36 weather
scenarios in the database, increasing the number of years simulated in the economic
dispatch (ED) module. These evolutions increase the overall ERAA robustness, as
well as the EVA robustness, given that the profitability of resources is evaluated
considering future climate changes.
Weather Scenario selection
(65) As in ERAA 2023, the EVA of ERAA 2024 models only three weather scenarios to
cope with computational complexity. This simplification introduces one of the key
inconsistencies in assumptions compared to the ED module, where a set of 36 weather
scenarios is used.
(66) ENTSO-E, incorporating ACER comments expressed during the ERAA 2024
development phase23, improved the method to select the three weather scenarios for
the EVA out of the entire set of 36 weather scenarios24. The method evaluates which
subset results in the greatest consistency with the ED. The criterion used to assess
representativeness is capacities’ revenue across all TY, ensuring that capacities
deemed (un)profitable in the EVA are evaluated the same way in the ED.
(67) ACER acknowledges the improvements in the selection method for the weather
scenarios. ACER considers that the selected subset of weather scenarios is in line with
the methodology introduced.
(68) In the advanced stages of the ERAA 2024 process, ENTSO-E had to recalibrate the
weights assigned to each selected weather scenario when running the EVA to increase
revenue consistency. From an equal weighting between weather scenarios, the relative
weights were changed.25 Although the recalibration increased the robustness of the
EVA, it created delays in the ERAA process, which indicates a necessary room for
improvement. For the future ERAA editions, ACER recommends that ENTSO-E
perform validation checks of the weather scenario selection before proceeding
23 ACER shared with ENTSO-E a note regarding the weather scenario selection methodology for the ERAA 2024
EVA and provided feedback in the bilateral meetings that followed.
24 The resulting selected subset of weather scenarios is WS14, WS25, and WS28 of the Climate Database.
25 Weights for scenarios WS14, WS25, and WS28 were changed to 0.439, 0.122, and 0.439, respectively.
Page 12 of 42

Decision No 07/2025
with the ERAA process. With such early checks, application of weather scenario
selection process can be more efficient and limit the delays.
(69) As assumptions regarding weather scenarios have a major impact on the ERAA
results, the target model should assume full consistency between the set of weather
scenarios in the EVA and those in the ED. ACER understands that, similarly to
previous ERAA editions and due to the modelling complexity, this was once again
not feasible. ACER notes that introducing the iterative revenues-based approach in
next ERAA cycles would solve this inconsistency, in case both modules would model
the same set of weather scenarios. Eventually, and despite the remaining room for
improvement, ACER acknowledges the robustness of the weather scenario selection
and considers the approach acceptable, as it improves consistency between the
modules of ERAA 2024. For future ERAA editions, ACER recommends that
consistency is further improved and that robustness checks are introduced
earlier in the selection process to avoid late modifications.
Forced outages
(70) Regarding forced outages on capacity resources, the ED runs with independent
random outages for each individual power plant, whereas the EVA uses aggregated
outage profiles per technology. For the EVA of ERAA 2024, ENTSO-E applied a
representative outage pattern based on a set of fifteen outage samples generated with
the same parameters as the ED forced outages. It constitutes an improvement on last
year’s approach of uniform capacity derating based on the average forced outage rate.
ACER welcomes the introduction of a dynamic forced outage pattern in the EVA, as
it better represents the dynamics of generators’ unavailability. This improvement
strengthens the consistency between the EVA and ED modules.
(71) However, the EVA-ED inconsistency in the modelling of forced outages of
interconnectors has not been resolved for interconnections modelled using net transfer
capacities (NTC).26 Regarding the interconnections where flow-based (FB) capacity
calculation applies, no additional forced outages are assumed to occur since outages
are already implicitly considered in the FB domains. Provided the FB domains are
consistent between the EVA and the ED, this approach guarantees full consistency in
the interconnector forced outage modelling. ACER notes that the introduction of the
iterative revenues-based approach for the EVA would likely solve these divergences,
and calls for further improvements in future ERAA editions.

Investment parameters and constraints
(72) ERAA 2024 considers decision options for commissioning, decommissioning, or
lifetime extension for gas.27 The same options – except for decommissioning – apply
to DSR and battery storage. Coal, lignite and oil power plants can only be
decommissioned or life extended. On the other hand, nuclear and renewable power
capacities are considered completely policy driven. While such an assumption is
largely valid for the nuclear, it is not fully applicable to renewable energy power
generation plants, some of which are already competitive.28
(73) ENTSO-E indicated that mothballing – and supposedly de-mothballing – could not be
considered in the EVA of ERAA 2024, arguing that the impact on the results is
expected to be minor. ACER notes that this assumption is not line with Article
23(5)(b) of the Electricity Regulation, and is a setback compared to previous ERAA
editions. ACER is unable to assess the impact of this simplification as it would require
re-running ERAA 2024 with (de)mothballing to compare results. For the benefit of
the ERAA 2024 process, ACER does not request these re-runs, yet it does request that
ENTSO-E reintegrate (de)mothballing in the future ERAA editions.
(74) The investment decision-making process is modelled using the weighted average cost
of capital (WACC) along with an additional estimated risk premium called hurdle rate.
While the WACC captures both country-specific and systemic risks, the hurdle rate
introduces an extra premium to reflect investors’ risk aversion. This premium
compensates for uncertainties such as potential regulatory interventions, such as price
caps, and the unpredictability of price spikes. ACER reviewed the approach used in
ERAA 2024 to model investors’ risk aversion and considers it adequate. ACER also
welcomes the methodological insights, greater data transparency, and additional
underlying assumption details that ENTSO-E provided in Section 10.11 of Annex II
of the ERAA 2024 Report.
(75) Whether capacities are being (de)commissioned or life extended in the EVA depends
on the balance between the costs and the cost-reduction that capacities bring to the
overall European power system. The costs used in the objective function of the EVA
are based on the cost of new entry (CONE) studies, conducted in accordance with the
European methodology29. National studies provide CONE values that reflect country-
specific economic conditions. For countries not conducting national studies, ENTSO-
E computes default values using the average of the available national CONE figures.
These CONE values are then used as inputs into the EVA to optimally size the
capacities of candidate technologies.

(76) ENTSO-E informed ACER that the initial EVA run of ERAA 2024 resulted in non-
intuitive commissioning outcomes, especially in the Belgian and German bidding
zones for 2035. Indeed, the initial run resulted in commissioning 9.66 GW30 of gas
(CCGT) in Belgium, without new gas units entering in Germany. ACER understands
that such effect is due to the absence of specified new entry potential (commissioning

default values assigned to Germany for gas units31. Investment in Belgium is
unrestricted while being considered economically optimal by the model; hence

(77) ENTSO-E conducted a rerun of the EVA with a single default value for each gas
technology (CCGT and OCGT) for all bidding zones to avoid spillover effects where
bidding zones with low CONE values would capture most new investments. The
single default values used the average of all national CONE figures32. ACER
acknowledges that some of the significant differences between national CONE values
between neighbouring countries for mature technologies (CCGT and OCGT) might
stem from diverging implementation of the European methodology. Nevertheless,
ACER notes that such an approach overrides the CONE inputs that capture national
specificities and removes the geographical optimisation of new commissioned
capacities. In effect, taking average values does not allow to capture the true dynamics
of the internal energy market which is the primary purpose of the European-level
assessment.
(78) Finally, ACER notes that the default values for capital costs have substantially
changed from ERAA 2023 to ERAA 2024 – an increase that appears difficult to justify
in a year-on-year comparison33. Considering that the default values are used for all
countries in ERAA 2024, resulting in a value increase for most of the bidding zones
modelled, the observed impact of this choice is an increase of the perceived adequacy
risks compared to ERAA 2023.

approach to technical and economic parameters used. To facilitate that and to
provide a constructive, long-lasting solution, ACER commissioned a study to

Curtailment sharing
(80) In its previous decisions, ACER highlighted substantial issues regarding the
modelling assumptions and the implementation of curtailment sharing. Essentially,
analysis of ERAA 2022 and ERAA 2023 results indicated that these were a key driver
for the inconsistencies between the ED and the EVA, substantially increasing
perceived adequacy risks in the ED module34. To assess the robustness of the
curtailment sharing implementation in ERAA, ACER requested ENTSO-E35 to
provide an analysis (case-study) focused on 1) demonstrating how curtailment sharing
brings ED results closer to market functioning, 2) reviewing curtailment sharing
implementation in ERAA, and 3) assessing the impact of trading after day-ahead
market coupling on curtailment sharing results36. Despite the critical importance of
such analysis, ENTSO-E has not submitted it yet37.
(81) In ERAA 2024, neither local matching38 nor curtailment sharing39 are part of the EVA
module (similar to ERAA 2023), which creates inconsistencies in the geographical
repartition and amount of scarcity situations in the EVA and in the ED modules.
ACER finds that curtailment sharing increases the number of shortage hours observed
in the ED by 132.4%40, and hence the number of hours with scarcity prices which
generators would benefit from41, while not being considered when assessing
capacities’ entries and exits. Additionally, the energy-not-served (ENS) redistribution
across bidding zones performed in the economic dispatch modifies the localisation of
scarcity situations, which is also not reflected in the EVA, likely leading to
underinvestment in certain Member States and, to a lesser extent, overinvestment in

Price cap assumptions
(86) ENTSO-E computed a new price cap evolution for each target year of ERAA 2024,
based on the initial SDAC price cap. For target years 2026, 2028, 2030, and 2035
respectively, the applied price caps are 4500, 5000, 6000, and 6500 EUR/MWh.
(87) ACER considers that the definition of the maximum clearing price in ERAA 2024, set
equal to the technical bidding limit in the day-ahead market, is not fully aligned with
Article 5(13) of the ERAA methodology and Article 10 (1) and (2) of the Electricity
Regulation. The maximum clearing price of the EVA should consider at least the
technical bidding limits of the day-ahead and intraday markets in conjunction, which
would increase price cap levels for all considered target years. The current
implementation limits the level of price spikes, which has a direct impact on the
economic viability of investments in new resources (e.g. for peaking capacities)43.
(88) ACER understands that the price cap evolution across target years is computed
following methodological changes introduced by ERAA 2023. The original intent of
the price evolution was to replicate the criteria and process for adjusting the
harmonised maximum and minimum clearing prices for SDAC44, inducing price cap
increases following repeated price spikes. As the dynamic price cap increases cannot
be modelled endogenously, a yearly evolution is based on the occurrence of price
spikes in the Economic Dispatch (ED) runs of the previous ERAA edition.
(89) This causality relation between price spikes in the previous ERAA edition and price
caps in the current ERAA edition bring certain concerns. As price spikes occur during
scarcity, the adequacy outcomes of the previous ERAA influence price caps levels of
the current ERAA, and, by extension, affect (de)commissioning or (de)mothballing
decisions45. A methodological approach based on interdependency between ERAA
outcomes is not justified in ACER’s view. ACER highlights a risk of cyclical
adequacy risks assessment, with ERAA versions alternating between resource
adequacy and resource scarcity46. ACER recommends that ENTSO-E investigate
the impact of the interdependency on the robustness of the ERAA results. Should

Flow-based domains
(90) As to reduce inconsistency between the EVA and the ED, the ERAA 2024 EVA now
models the market coupling of the modelled zones using the flow-based approach
instead of the net transferred capacities (NTC). Since the former is also used in the
economic dispatch module, such modelling improvement reduces differences between
the two modules. Nonetheless, the implementation of the flow-based domains is still
lacking. There are six flow-based domains modelled in the ED module per target year.
However, only two domains are used in the EVA. Such discrepancy creates
inconsistencies between the EVA and the ED, influencing entry and exit decisions in
the model. ACER recommends that ENTSO-E gradually improve FB domains
consistency in the upcoming ERAA 2025, notably through by shifting towards a
revenues-based approach.
French nuclear availability profiles
(91) In ERAA 2024, ENTSO-E added additional French nuclear availability profiles to the
reference set used in previous ERAA editions for 2030 and 2035: a ‘Low Availability’
profile and a ‘High Availability’ profile. ENTSO-E suggests that these profiles better
capture the dispersion of nuclear generation in France.
(92) According to ENTSO-E, both profiles aim to replicate the dynamics of French nuclear
availability, i.e., higher availability in winter months and lower availability in summer
months, similar to the reference set. Compared to the nuclear reference availability
case, the High and Low Availability profile represent, respectively, optimistic and
pessimistic scenarios for French nuclear availability during the winter months.
(93) ACER observes that, overall, the High Availability profile47 reflects an availability of
the French fleet which is relatively close to the upper bound of the reference set
(consisting of 15 forced outage scenarios), notably during the winder period. The
decreasing impact of the runs with the higher French nuclear availability on adequacy
outcomes is relatively limited compared to the reference nuclear availability scenario,
with LOLE on average decreasing from 1.79h to 1.63h for 2030 and 4.95h to 4.02h
for 2035 in France, and from 9.36h to 7.96h for 2035 in Belgium. For 2030 in
Belgium, LOLE is even slightly increasing from 3.76h to 3.77h.
(94) For the Low Availability profile, ACER notes that the nuclear availability in winter
months (from December to February) has been capped at 39 GW. Such assumption
represents a rare case of nuclear unavailability, with the historic lowest average

profiles is equivalent to considering that the French nuclear fleet will be affected by a
“corrosion crisis” once every three years. Although ACER acknowledges the
importance of accounting for such extreme cases in probabilistic assessment, such
weighting assumption is highly pessimistic and should be considered as an adverse
case assessment.
(96) ACER considers that the introduction of the two additional French nuclear availability
profiles should have a strong impact on adequacy results across the neighbouring
zones. This can be particularly expected, as France historically has been a net exporter
averaging 47.66 TWh of annual exports from 2005 to 202450. Only during the
corrosion crisis in 2022, France turned into a net importer at -16.5 TWh. However,
this dynamic is not reflected in ERAA 2024, as only the French and Belgian bidding
zones adequacy results were reported with the additional French nuclear scenarios.
Hence, ACER considers that the impact of the Low Availability Profile on cross-
border exchanges with the remaining neighbours is not considered.
(97) The two additional nuclear availability profiles were introduced in the ED and not in
the EVA, resulting in a significant inconsistency between modules. This diverging
approach influences entry and exit decisions, as different French nuclear availability
profiles are bound to impact the optimal dispatch of capacities, hence their revenues.
Although certain modelling simplifications are acceptable when running the EVA to
limit computational complexity, changes to the underlying assumptions of nuclear
availability cannot be considered acceptable. ACER considers that ENTSO-E should
have maintained the French nuclear availability consistency between the EVA and the

Conclusions
(104) Compared to ERAA 2023, ENTSO-E’s 2024 assessment applies new approaches to
specific modelling areas including the modelled years, time resolution, representation
of investment costs and climate years, and electricity exchange constraints. These
changes were designed to improve consistency between the EVA and the ED module.
Indeed, they strengthened the robustness of the ERAA implementation. There is still,
however, a significant room for improvement, especially in the areas indicated above.
Moreover, to ensure reliable results in the future, the improvements must form a
coherent structural fix that provides a lasting solution eliminating the inconsistency
issue. ACER considers that the methodological approaches used in the EVA are, in
principle, acceptable for the purpose of ERAA 2024. However, the consistency level
with the ED module regarding revenues and adequacy metrics, which curtailment
sharing and its implementation appear to have a significant impact on, cannot be
accepted with the current available information.

6.2.6. Storage
(105) Article 23(5)(d) of the Electricity Regulation requires that the assessment
appropriately takes account of the contribution of energy storage, including its
contribution to flexible system operation. Article 23(5)(m) of the Electricity
Regulation also requires that the national characteristics of energy storage are properly
taken into consideration. These requirements are reflected in Article 4 of the ERAA
methodology.
(106) ERAA 2024 applies the same approach for the consideration of pumped-storage hydro
and battery storage as previous ERAAs. Regarding battery storage, capacities are
distinguished between market batteries (modelled explicitly) and non-market batteries
(exogeneous to the model and integrated in the demand profile).55 As in ERAA 2023,
the approach to optimising the use of storage is broadly consistent with the Electricity
Regulation and the ERAA methodology, and ACER considers that ERAA 2024
sufficiently reflects the applicable requirements of the Electricity Regulation
regarding the consideration of storage in the assessment56. Further improvements are
needed in future ERAA editions with regards to modelling the economics of storage
assets. In real life, storage resources capture various revenue streams (such as
balancing capacity revenues), which should be accounted for in the model.
Furthermore, as battery storage becomes a mature technology (and its deployment
subject to economic signals) the ERAA model should properly reflect this and allow
the EVA to both commission and decommission battery storage, applying no upper
limits on storage expansion (other than technical limitations).
6.2.7. Cross-zonal capacities
(107) According to Article 23(5) of the Electricity Regulation, ERAA must properly take
into consideration the level of interconnection, interconnection targets, real network
development, and be based on a market model using the flow-based capacity
calculation approach, where applicable. ERAA shall also appropriately considers the
contribution of imports and exports to adequacy, by accurately reflecting the capacity
calculation approach used on each bidding zone’s border. These requirements are
further specified in Articles 3 and 4 of the ERAA methodology. Further details on
ACER’s assessment of the cross-zonal capacities are provided in the chapter 4 of
Annex III to this Decision.
(108) Regarding interconnectivity and assumed network developments from 2026 onwards
in the ED module, ERAA 2024 uses differentiated flow-based domains for every

both the economic dispatch module and the EVA, a change from the previous editions.
For the Nordic CCR, ERAA 2024 introduces the flow-based approach for the risk
module for the first time, whereas the EVA continues with the NTC approach. The
flow-based approach allows to optimise the use of cross-border capacities and better
capture situations of simultaneous scarcity among bidding zones compared to the NTC
approach. On the other hand, without applying flow-based approach in the EVA, the
investment module may not properly reflect the optimised (higher) cross-border
contributions considering non-scarcity situations among the Nordic CCR zones.
(110) ACER welcomes applying the flow-based approach in the ED and EVA modules for
the Core CCR as an improvement to increase consistency in ERAA 2024. For the ED
module ENTSO-E has increased the number of flow-based domains to six per target
year. To guarantee the necessary consistency between EVA and ED, both modules
should use the same number of flow-based domains. However, due to computational
complexity, the EVA module in ERAA 2024 is using only two flow-based domains
per target year. In this case, proper representation of the set of the ED domains when
reducing the number of FB domains for the EVA is important. In ERAA 2024,
ENTSO-E has followed clustering techniques used in the past editions with the
objective to reduce the number of FB domains from 8760 (for one year) to 6 for the
ED and 2 in the EVA. According to an analysis carried out by the Joint Research
Centre (see Annex III of this Decision) comparing the set of flow-based domains used
to the ED with the set of flow-based domains used to the EVA, it appears that the
reduced set of flow-based domains maintains a reasonable level of consistency with
the six flow-based domains used in the ED. For next year’s ERAA ENTSO-E should
strive to use the same set of flow-based domains between modules to ensure full
consistency between cross-zonal capacities in the two models of the ERAA. If the
alternative option of using a reduced set of flow-based domains in the EVA is again
followed, full representation of the reduced set of flow-based domains in the EVA
with the flow-based domains in the ED should be ensured. (see more in section 8).
(111) Regarding NTC-based calculations reflecting the minimum 70% target in ERAA
2024, the Report does not provide sufficient information on how the compliance is
ensured. As with previous ERAA editions, ACER highlights that ENTSO-E needs to
ensure compliance with the 70% target for all borders and improve the transparency
on this topic in subsequent ERAAs.
(112) Overall, ACER acknowledges the improvements made in ERAA 2024, compared to
ERAA 2023, with regard to the relevant requirements of the Electricity Regulation

6.2.8. Demand, DSR and sectoral integration
(113) Article 23(5) of the Electricity Regulation requires that ERAA is based on appropriate
central reference scenarios of projected demand, accounting for the contribution of
DSR and sectoral integration and properly reflecting the national characteristics of
demand flexibility. Article 4 of the ERAA methodology further specifies these
requirements, particularly stating that ERAA shall consider both implicit and explicit
DSR. Further details on ACER’s assessment of the DSR assumptions are provided in
the chapter 3 of Annex III to this Decision.
(114) While ACER notes a general upward trend in projected demand, it emphasises a
particularly sharp 15% increase as soon as 2026 compared with the demand level
observed in 2024 and 2025.57 In its reaction to the consulted ERAA 2024 preliminary
input data, ACER also highlighted substantial demand growth projections compared
to their 2023 levels in some zones (up to 12% per year58). ACER also notes that despite
the recommendations included in the public consultation feedback, demand
projections were not scaled back. Instead, an additional significant demand increase
was observed in Germany.59
(115) ACER repeats that in the interest of transparency, the national demand projections
should be accompanied by the general assumptions used by the TSOs, indicating per
sector electricity demand increases and the reasons behind demand reductions.60
Greater transparency regarding the modelling tools used by ENTSO-E or the TSOs to
produce demand projections and profiles is essential, particularly given the impact of
demand assumptions on ERAA outcomes. For future ERAAs, ACER recommends
that ENTSO-E shares detailed explanations of the tools’ functioning, which
would significantly improve stakeholders’ ability to understand demand
projections and meaningfully contribute to their creation.
(116) The approach applied to demand flexibility in ERAA 2024 is similar as in ERAA
2023, that is, modelling in three categories: explicit DSR, price-insensitive implicit
DSR, and price-sensitive implicit DSR. Each category is modelled as follows:

6.2.10. Probabilistic assessment
(124) Article 23(5) of the Electricity Regulation requires that ERAA applies probabilistic
calculations and includes at least the two indicators – LOLE and EENS. These
requirements are further specified in Article 4 of the ERAA methodology.
(125) ERAA 2024 uses the same probabilistic approach to assess the risks to resource
adequacy as ERAA 2023, ERAA 2022 and ERAA 2021. The approach aims at
capturing the uncertainty associated with future weather conditions and the
availability of generation and interconnection assets, though the Monte-Carlo
simulations. A key output of these simulations are the probabilistic risk indicators of
LOLE and EENS.
(126) As with last year, ERAA 2024 did not report the exact value of the threshold used to
evaluate the evolution of the convergence criteria in Annex 3 of the Report but rather
provided a graphical representation to prove that the results of the model are stable
enough. Given the stability of coefficient 𝛼 showcased in Figure 6 of Annex 3 of the
report and its consistency over time (compared to previous ERAA), ACER considers
that ENTSO-E made sufficient proof of the convergence of the model.
(127) ACER considers this probabilistic assessment approach in ERAA 2024 is in line with
the requirements. Nevertheless, ACER believes that by providing an exact threshold
value, ENTSO-E could increase the transparency.
6.2.11. Single modelling tool
(128) Article 23(5) of the Electricity Regulation requires that the assessment applies a single
modelling tool, which is then reflected in Article 4 of the ERAA methodology.
(129) Since ERAA 2024 uses the same modelling tool for all target years, it meets the
requirement of the Electricity Regulation for a single modelling tool.

6.2.12. Out-of-market capacity resources
(130) Article 23(5) of the Electricity Regulation requires that the assessment appropriately
considers the contribution of all resources. In this respect, Article 7(10) of the ERAA
methodology requires that the assessment projects the risks in the absence of any out-
of-market capacity resources and after their activation.74 Moreover, pursuant to
Article 8(1) of the ERAA methodology, the assessment can only identify resource
adequacy concerns after considering the impacts of out-of-market resources.
(131) ERAA 2024 offers more insights on the impact of out-of-market measures, an
improvement from the previous ERAA. In a couple of Member States, namely
Germany, Ireland, Luxembourg, and Malta, adequacy metrics are shown both with
and without the activation of out-of-market measures.75. The two sets of results allow
to assess how these measures help alleviate adequacy concerns76. Additionally, in one
Member State, Poland, where DSR procured under the capacity mechanism is used as
an out-of-market measure during scarcity periods, but the results are only presented
after the activation of this measure77.
(132) ACER welcomes the increased transparency regarding out-of-market measures
and considers it good practice, but recommends that their impact on resource
adequacy be presented for all affected countries. Additionally, ACER
recommends that the next ERAA accounts for all relevant out-of-market
capacity resources that are present across the EU Member States78 Overall,
ACER acknowledges the improvement ENTSO-E introduced to the approach
regarding out-of-market resources in ERAA 2024 and considers the implementation
acceptable for the 2024 edition.
6.2.13. Identification of sources of resource adequacy concerns
(133) Article 23(5)(k) of the Electricity Regulation requires that the assessment identifies
the sources of possible resource adequacy concerns, whether it is a network constraint,
a resource constraint, or both. Article 8 of the ERAA methodology elaborates on the
potential drivers to be assessed, including methodological approaches for carrying out
the assessment.
74 Out-of-market capacity resources are resources that lie outside the market, i.e. do not participate in the
wholesale market, that TSOs would only use as a last resort if the market fails to meet electricity demand.

has 0.175 GW emergency gas oil-fired back-up plants, for risk preparedness.

(134) Similar to past editions, the Report gathers various insights from the TSOs concerning
the national context, often explaining the assumptions, sources of adequacy risks or
other considerations included in Annex IV of the Report titled Country Comments.
(135) The submitted Report does not include the analysis of the sources of scarcity nor their
correlation with demand and supply. ACER considers that ERAA should feature a
clear identification of the concerns’ sources for each of the relevant Member States.
The need for this addition is exemplified by substantial increase of adequacy risks
observed in ERAA 2024 compared to the last year’s edition.
(136) To deepen the understanding of the correlation of scarcity situations with system
conditions in ERAA 2024, as well as to gain insights into the evolution of adequacy
risks compared to previous ERAA editions, ACER requested ENTSO-E to explain the
reasons of scarcities in each bidding zones or Member States in ERAA 2024.
Specifically, the following information was requested:
i. An explanation of the different geographical distribution of ENS events in
ERAA 2024 compared to ERAA 2023, notably why LOLE is mostly
concentrated in the CORE and Nordic Region whereas peripherical bidding
zones concentrate adequacy risks in ERAA 2023.
ii. An explanation, for each Member States, of adequacy risks evolutions
compared to ERAA 2023.
iii. An analysis of the correlations of scarcity situations with system conditions,
namely, demand levels, generation availability, and cross-border exchanges
during scarcity.
(137) Regarding the geographical distribution of scarcities, ENTSO-E clarified that the key
elements to consider are changes in demand profiles and the use of flow-based
domains. First, according to ENTSO-E, ERAA 2024 indicates a rise in demand,
particularly in the CORE region. This growth trend continues steadily beyond 2030.
Additionally, the increase in demand growth is not evenly distributed throughout the
year; it is concentrated in the winter months for several countries with the CORE
region being especially affected. Second, the comparison of the CORE region results
may also be impacted by the improved application of flow-based domains in ERAA
2024. More robust and accurate estimates of cross-zonal exchanges in the region could
drive some differences between the two ERAA versions.
(138) ENTSO-E provided an analysis of the correlation of scarcity situations and system
conditions. ENTSO-E did not provide a detailed explanation regarding the evolution
of adequacy risks for each Member State. ACER understands that the short timeline
may have made it difficult for ENTSO-E to perform this analysis.
(139) ACER considers that, with the detailed information regarding scarcity situations and
explanation regarding the geographical shift of risks, ERAA 2024 represents a
meaningful improvement over the previous edition. Still, additional efforts are
necessary for the next ERAA edition to enhance the identification of risk drivers.

6.2.14. Transparency
(140) Article 41(2) of the Electricity Regulation requires that ENTSO-E operates in full
transparency towards its stakeholders. Article 11 of the ERAA methodology sets out
requirements ensuring that ERAA is a transparent assessment and that the Report
facilitates stakeholders’ understanding of the assessment, including inputs, data,
assumptions and scenario development.
(141) Overall, transparency in ERAA 2024 remained on the similar level as in the last year’s
ERAA. ACER is of the view that there is still a considerable scope to enhance the
assessment’s transparency going forward, with particular focus on making the Report
more accessible to diverse stakeholders.
(142) In terms of data availability, ERAA 2024 shows progress in meeting transparency
requirements, representing an improvement compared to last years’ editions. Notably,
the Report contains hourly energy not served data, refined methodological
descriptions and separate outcomes with and without out of market measures, as
described in section 6.2.12. Moreover, the input data and the modelling outcomes are
publicly available through interactive dashboards, enhancing both the accessibility
and clarity of the dataset79.
(143) At the same time, ACER considers that further data items should be made public to
allow better interpretation of the results80. To ensure full transparency, relevant data
should be made available for stakeholders and the general public, to facilitate their
understanding of the outcome.
(144) Regarding curtailment sharing, ACER acknowledges ENTSO-E’s efforts to clarify
the functioning and implementation of curtailment sharing in response to the request
for information. Nonetheless, this improved clarity is not reflected in the ERAA 2024
report, hence not made available to Member States, stakeholders, and the general
public to assess the robustness of the feature. ACER recommends that ENTSO-E
further improves transparency in the next ERAA report, specifically in the
methodological annex.
(145) Additionally, pre-curtailment sharing outcomes are not publicly available despite the
high impact of the feature on adequacy results. ACER recommends including these
intermediary results in the next ERAA editions, as it would significantly improve
transparency.
(146) The Report includes a description of the results and of the methodology and
assumptions considered in the assessment. At the same time, the Report does not

Assessment of the procedural requirements
6.3.1. Timeline for submission
(150) ERAA 2024 complies with Article 23(2) of the Electricity Regulation, which requires
annual assessments. However, it was submitted to ACER on 7 April 2025, resulting
in a significant delay of 178 days beyond the 1 November deadline set in Article 10(2)
of the ERAA methodology. This delay was partly due to ENTSO-E exploring multiple
modelling and scenario variants, addressing data quality issues, and implementing
late-stage changes. ENTSO-E informed ACER of the delay in advance. To prevent
impacts on processes relying on ERAA, such as State aid approvals, such significant
delays should be avoided in future ERAA cycles.
6.3.2. Data collection requirements
(151) Article 23(4) of the Electricity Regulation requires the TSOs to provide ENTSO-E
with the data it needs to carry out ERAA. Article 5 and Article 10 of the ERAA
methodology specify that ENTSO-E must provide the TSOs with data collection
guidelines to ensure coherency of the input data across the assessment and publish
these guidelines.
(152) As in the past two years, ENTSO-E has collected data from the TSOs for ERAA 2024,
as for example evidenced by the surveys run on the assumptions of the assessment
with the TSOs and has published the data collection guidelines alongside the Report.
ERAA 2024 is therefore in line with these requirements.
6.3.3. Stakeholder engagement
(153) Article 31 and Article 30(1)(c) of the Electricity Regulation require that ENTSO-E
conducts an extensive consultation process and takes into consideration stakeholders’
comments when finalising the annual ERAAs. Article 27 of the Electricity Regulation
requires ENTSO-E to consult relevant stakeholders and duly take the results of that
consultation into consideration. The requirements are further specified in Article 9 of
the ERAA methodology and aim to enable stakeholders to contribute at every step of
developing ERAA in a way that is transparent, open, accessible, inclusive, efficient
and well-structured. Article 23(7) of the Electricity Regulation requires that ERAA is
subject to the prior consultation of Member States, the ECG and relevant stakeholders
before it is submitted to ACER for approval.
(154) ENTSO-E consulted stakeholders on the preliminary scenario assumptions and held
public webinars to inform stakeholders about methodological approaches and
developments for ERAA 2024. In total, ENTSO-E held three public webinars to
inform stakeholders and seek feedback.82 In addition, ENTSO-E consulted on the
82 For more information, on ENTSO-E’s stakeholder engagement activities, see ENTSO-E’s dedicated webpage
on ERAA.

preliminary results with the ECG. ACER also recognises ENTSO-E’s commitment to

(155) Nevertheless, ACER observed that, as in 2023, ENTSO-E introduced or amended a
number of input data (e.g. climate years, values for cost of new entry, French nuclear
availability patterns) after the consultation on scenario assumptions without duly

(156) ACER also notes that ENTSO-E did not explicitly react to the stakeholder feedback.
While stakeholder replies to public consultations were published83, ENTSO did not

Therefore, it is unclear how these comments were considered in finalising the

(157) In summary, ACER recognises ENTSO-E’s efforts to engage stakeholder on the

in Section 6.2.5, ACER raises concerns about the lack of consultation regarding
additional nuclear scenarios. More broadly, ACER does not consider it appropriate to
introduce last-minute changes that were not previously discussed. Hence, while the
overall stakeholder engagement has been acceptable for ERAA 2024, improvements
could still be made in particular to avoid last minute non-consulted changes.
