(159) In Table 1, five out of 17 aspects of ERAA analysed by ACER are marked “green”
indicating that they comply with the applicable requirements.
(160) Most of the analysed aspects are marked “yellow”. This indicates that, for the 2024
edition, these are considered acceptable methodological simplifications, as their
impact is deemed sufficiently limited and do not compromise the suitability of ERAA
2024 for informing policy decisions.
(161) One aspect is marked “grey” indicating that ACER is not able to assess it due to
insufficient information provided in the Report.
(162) While ERAA 2024 still includes several methodological simplifications that require
further refinement, ACER considers that they do not compromise the results of the
central reference scenario in the identification of resource adequacy concerns. At the
same time, ACER acknowledges that these aspects are not yet fully in line with the
requirements of the Electricity Regulation and the ERAA methodology. This
conclusion is without prejudice to ACER’s future assessments, as similar
simplifications in future ERAAs may be assessed differently given the evolving nature
of electricity markets and the ERAA model itself. The same modelling choices made
in future ERAAs may impact the robustness of their results differently than in 2024.
(163) ACER also recognises that the implementation of the ERAA methodology has come
a long way: gradually improving every year thanks to the dedication of many ENTSO-
E and TSO experts. With this momentum and the ongoing efforts of the
implementation team, ACER expects ERAA to become a benchmark for adequacy
monitoring and supporting policy decisions across Europe.
(164) Given the complexity of the ERAA exercise, it is challenging to introduce substantial
amendments within the three-month decision period. This highlights the importance
of ongoing informal communication and consensus-building with ENTSO-E during
the development phase. Unilateral non-consulted changes with potentially defining
impact on the results, especially those introduced late in the process, can hamper
effective regulatory oversight.
ACER’s amendments and reasons
(165) ACER considers that four elements require amendments to ensure that ERAA 2024 is
both robust and compliant with the regulatory framework.
Page 37 of 42

Decision No 07/2025
(166) First, ACER considers it necessary to amend ERAA 2024 Report by including the
missing results for all Italian and Maltese bidding zones for target year 2026. This is
to ensure compliance of ERAA 2024 with the required geographical scope of the
assessment, as explained in Section 6.2.1.
(167) Second, the information explaining the functioning and implementation of curtailment
sharing which was provided by ENTSO-E upon ACER’s request referred to in Section
6.2.5. should be included in the ERAA 2024 Report. ACER has therefore amended
Section 11.7. of Annex 2 of the Report by incorporating this information in the
description of the adequacy patch of the EUPHEMIA algorithm and its replication in
ERAA and removing descriptions which are inconsistent with this information.
(168) Additionally, considering that the curtailment sharing feature, on average, nearly
doubles adequacy risks as reported in Section 6.2.5., ACER considers it necessary to
amend Annex 3 of the Report by including pre-curtailment sharing outcomes to ensure
transparency. The additional data provide insight on how the curtailment sharing
feature redistributes ENS across bidding zones and how it impacts adequacy risks in
each of them. Also, ACER is of the view that publishing the additional data in the
Report itself rather than in the annexes of ACER’s decision, as it was done for
previous ERAA editions, is more transparent and improves the accessibility of the
data to stakeholders. However, ACER notes that these pre-curtailment outcomes are
not final ERAA results and are provided solely to ensure transparency.
(169) Third, the two additional French nuclear profiles represent a promising direction
towards better representation of the availability of specific types of resources in
ERAA. However, as explained Section 6.2.5., the last-minute incorporation of the
profiles into ERAA 2024 raises concerns, both regarding the process and the profiles
themselves. Regarding the process, the profiles were not consulted with the
stakeholders nor discussed with ACER and the regulatory authorities. They were also
introduced exclusively in the adequacy module, creating a significant inconsistency
between investment signals and actual scarcity situations. Additionally, the low
availability profile should qualify as an adverse case assessment, especially given the
low, flat shaped, availability curve over the winter season that represents a situation
that is worse than the one experienced in the “corrosion crisis” of 2022/23. The
weighting given to these results in the final ERAA results is so high that would be
comparable to experiencing a “corrosion crisis” every third year. Lastly, the submitted
Report only accounts for the effects of additional profiles for French and Belgian
resource adequacy, despite the obvious implications of the limited French nuclear
output for other Member States.
(170) While acknowledging the ongoing shift towards a better representation of resource
outages, ACER does not consider this approach to be mature enough for the pan-
European assessment. Therefore, ACER deems it necessary to amend ERAA 2024 by
retaining only the reference availability modelling outcomes in the central reference
scenario. At the same time, the outcome of modelling additional nuclear profiles
should be considered in the Report as a proof of concept that would support the
interpretation of the ERAA adequacy outcomes. This proof of concept should
encourage further work and discussions around outage modelling in ERAA.
Page 38 of 42

Decision No 07/2025
(171) Fourth, ACER believes that the policymakers, regulatory authorities and the general
public should be supported in their interpretation of the ERAA 2024 results. To this
end, ENTSO-E’s identification of the sources of adequacy concerns described in
Section 6.2.13. should complement the outcomes reported in Annex 3: Detailed
Results. The report should offer descriptive statistics and explanation in addition to
the key results of loss of load probability and expected energy not served. It should
also bring further insights into simulated scarcities that allow for better understanding
of the root causes and inform necessary steps to address them.
(172) Hence, ACER considers it necessary to amend Annex 3 of the submitted ERAA 2024
Report by including ENTSO-E’s detailed information about scarcity situations for
each concerned Member State. Without these explanations, ERAA 2024 would not
meet the requirement of Article 23(5)(k) of the Electricity Regulation and would also
be less helpful for decision-makers and stakeholders trying to interpret its results
accurately. This information should at least shed light on the correlation of scarcity
situations with demand, supply, and cross-border exchanges and provide insights on
why adequacy risks of ERAA 2024 are higher and differently geographically
distributed than those of ERAA 2023. Furthermore, the Executive Summary should
clarify the consideration of out-of-market measures.
(173) The precise wording of the above amendments is included in Annex II to this
Decision, published by ACER. In the interest of ensuring clarity and to prevent
potential confusion among stakeholders, ACER requests that ENTSO-E promptly
revise the draft ERAA Report published on ENTSO-E’s website in April 2025 and
included in this Decision in Annexes I.a-I.g, to incorporate these amendments.
8. KEY RECOMMENDATIONS FOR ERAA 2025
(174) The annual ERAA assessments have undergone considerable gradual methodological
improvement over the past years. Since the first ERAA in 2021, ACER has been
assisting ENTSO-E in the implementation process by providing recommendations for
future ERAA editions. This section highlights key areas for further improvement.
(175) Similar to last year’s edition, ERAA 2024 has improved the key modelling aspects,
increasing the consistency of the modelling approaches, yet some of these
improvements are of temporary nature. These temporary solutions may be acceptable
in ERAA 2024, but they need to be further developed in the next ERAA edition to
form a coherent permanent solution. This would entail revising some crucial aspects
to align the modelling between the two modules:
(176) Regarding the consistent use of climate data and flow-based domains, currently the
ED module uses 36 weather scenarios and 6 domains per target year, while the EVA
module still relies on smaller subsets, that is 3 weather scenarios and 2 domains. As
any data reduction comes with information loss, consistency can only be guaranteed
if adequately similar weather scenarios and flow-based domains are used in both
ERAA modules.
Page 39 of 42

Decision No 07/2025
(177) Regarding a consistent approach to forced outage profiles, the availability of resources
has a significant effect on the adequacy results. The modelling approach should be
aligned in the ED and EVA modules, in particular for interconnectors, given their
disproportionate effect on modelled zones with low interconnectivity.
(178) When closing the residual consistency gap between the EVA and ED, two mutually
reinforcing metrics should be used: consistency of revenues and adequacy results.
Aligning both across the ERAA modules should remain a priority for ENTSO-E.
ACER recommends significant improvements in terms of such alignment in the next
ERAA. In particular, ACER recommends that the consistency in ERAA 2025
improves by at least 50% compared to ERAA 2024
(179) A definite move from temporary fixes to structural solutions also includes the
approach to technical and economic parameters used in ERAA. In ERAA 2024 this
was the most visible regarding the lack of stable values for investment costs for all
Member States and technologies. To offer a constructive, lasting solution, ACER
procured a study to provide these values and recommends ENTSO-E to use them in
the upcoming ERAA 2025 edition.
(180) Given its substantial impact on the final results, ACER considers it critical that no
uncertainty surrounding the curtailment sharing feature remains in future ERAA
reports. In that sense, the functioning of this patch should be extensively explained to
the stakeholders, proven as theoretically and empirically robust, and consistently
improved to ensure that it serves its purpose.
(181) Regarding scenario framework, ACER recommends that ENTSO-E include the “with
capacity mechanism” central reference scenario in the ERAA 2025 edition.
Considering the impact of capacity mechanisms on security of supply in the Member
States where they already exist, this scenario is highly relevant for policymakers.
(182) The modelling of DSR remains a subject of concerns as in previous ERAA editions.
ACER recommends ENTSO-E to focus on significant improvements in this area. On
the one hand, it should improve the input assumptions, such as addressing the
persistently low capacity assumed by the TSOs and the lack of transparency behind
the national estimates. On the other hand, it should also introduce changes in how
DSR is modelled in ERAA, including that it reflects realistic operation of load-shifting
resources, as described in Section 6.2.8.
(183) To ensure clarity and prevent any potential misunderstandings, all documents
published prior to the ACER approval of the ERAA should be explicitly labelled as
“Draft” or “Proposal”. This label should be displayed in a prominent manner to clarify
the preliminary status of the documents. Furthermore, given the ERAA annual
publication cycle, ACER recommends that all the future editions include a clear and
comprehensive information, e.g. in form of a release note, enabling the public to easily
assess changes and developments between the current and previous editions.
(184) It is important that ENTSO-E continues to engage with ACER throughout the
development of the annual ERAAs. This is the time when implementation choices and

their likely effect on the robustness of results can be discussed and, if needed, trade-
offs can be transparently evaluated. Ultimately, close cooperation with ACER during

(185) Current developments in the framework governing the internal electricity market86
brings new challenges and dynamics. The work to streamline the ERAA methodology
is underway and will shape the future ERAA cycles. Moreover, the ERAA
implementation improvements need consider that the ERAA reports will provide the
basis for the future assessments of flexibility needs.
(186) ACER acknowledges the complexity of the ERAA exercise and commends the
continued efforts of ENTSO-E and the TSO community. It is essential that ENTSO-
E continues to receive the necessary support and resources to deliver on this ambitious
task and continues the implementation in line with ACER recommendations. ACER
is confident that with ENTSO-E’s sustained dedication, the ERAA will increasingly
serve as a cornerstone of the EU’s coordinated adequacy framework.
